speaker: interviewer
text: She ran with that paper back. I don't know even if that woman she lived with-- they looked after her-- if they could read him that piece of paper. She could read Wanda [? Grobeminske, ?] my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, look, mom is alive. Mom is alive. They said, no, it's not your mother. Who is it? A piece of paper. Yes, yes. No, my mom is alive. It's mom, mommy's name. It's here. You read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came home. He worked with the police. He said to him-- maybe he could read Polish. He said, yes. That's my wife. She's under-- it's an amazing story-- under different name.

speaker: interviewer
text: She doesn't want me at all. She want only you. That was not your daddy. Why? She thought it's her father. She has another husband, maybe. Because she's not looking for me. She looks only for you. In the paper, I wrote [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. So it's means he went out and asked people, what is that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? What means [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? They told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came and said, no. If she is like that, she doesn't want me. I am not her husband. She doesn't want me. She wants only you. And we don't want her.

speaker: interviewee
text: You not-- you will not see your mother. You will be with me. And I don't want her, because she has another husband. He had two friends. They were in the Partisans, two big blokes, tall ones. I put their address. Please, if you live, if you are somewhere there, let me know, please. I had night duty in the ambulance there. I wasn't paid there too, only with food. At night duty, for a minute, I went in and sat down. They woke me middle of the night whatever they needed for, even nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Someone knocks at my door. I open-- I didn't open straight away. I said, who is there? If you would know Yiddish, if you would know who is here, you would open straight away. Who are you? Behind the door, I was afraid of them. They were all kinds of people, criminals too. I said, what is it? Did you give an advertisement in the Polish paper that you look for your daughter? Yes. I opened the door. Two men came in. And like from a-- from a mental home, they started to laugh, Poles, without a word, without introducing themselves, started to laugh. They laughed, and laughed, and didn't stop. And I said, look, if you need a doctor, why did you come to me? They are next door. Go to the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's not that. You said you had a child, yes, in the paper. And she is with your husband. But you are not your husband's wife. So I said, I know that. I know. My husband's not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he says that you're his wife and you have another husband. Where is your husband? I said, I haven't got a husband. So if you're alone, we will take you to your husband. All right, take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew everything. I knew in the beginning, that's not as they said. It's was winter. I was inside. I think I had a nighty on. I took a coat over barefoot in winter. I didn't think of dressing now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went and knocked on the door to the doctor and said, could you please give me some money? I have no money with me. What happened? News from my daughter. They knew the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me money. And he said, don't worry. Go as long as you need. There was no other nurse. Later, we had someone. And go there. Go [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. These two men took me. It was a long journey to [? Cassel. ?] It's some way from there over the other side. I came in. They knew when the train arrives. And they knew that I am there. He waited for his wife. Who has-- they couldn't-- no telephones. They couldn't phone him that I am alone-- his wife who has another husband who came for her child, the wife who I want from him, from my husband, the child. That what he thought. As I came in, it's like in a film. I nearly fell faint. Like, a stone wall, hundreds and hundreds-- like in a film, I didn't know what they want. Came across to me. And I was alone. These two men disappeared. They didn't want to tell the story, because they said it would have a battle, because they knew his wife. And they saw that I am not his wife. These two men who came to me, they were his friends. That's why he sent them to me. And they knew his wife who was killed. And they saw that I am entirely different person. I am not his wife. And he waited for his wife. And they thought straight away, maybe they tried-- if she looks like me, it's my daughter, not his. And he will not give her to me. Because he thinks-- maybe he was stupid, I don't know-- it's his child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So quickly, he left me. he went over to him and said, she is not your wife. Why do you want her? Why do you think that she has another husband? She is not your wife. [? Sura, ?] or what's her name-- she is not [? Sura. ?] We knew her. And they came all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quickly, quickly he changed. He don't want if I am not the wife that in all those people-- I don't know how many came-- in my eyes, they were thousands on thousands of people-- came across like a wall of people. And I was standing here, little, and waiting for my child. Suddenly, not he-- I hadn't seen him-- these people came to me. Look, we have heard your story. You're not his wife. And he says that the child is his. You say the child is yours. So the best thing is we make a wedding here. And I said, I didn't come for a wedding. And I didn't come for a husband. And I don't know him. Because he has the same name as I. I don't want him. I want my child only. And I want to see her. If she's not my child, I won't take away from him, if she's his, not mine. But I know she's my child, not his. I want my-- he won't give her to you until you marry him so the child will have a mother and a father. I said, yes. It's very nice. But I didn't come for a wedding here. And I don't want to get married at all. I want only my child, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you will never get her. So I want to see him if he's mad. Now I changed all politics. And I said, maybe he's a relative. So I will talk to him. I don't know him. I never knew anyone in [? Wofundermer. ?] But maybe he's a relative. So I just wanted to soften it.

speaker: interviewee
text: One of the women took me in. I was tired from the journey, tired from all what I lived through. And I fell on bed, on a bed there, and fell asleep. Not eating a whole day, we traveled-- I fell in bed and well, I fell asleep. Suddenly, it was I think Autumn coat-- suddenly, someone tore off. But how-- you know-- quick tore off my coverings and looked at my face. And that was him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I opened my face with my eyes, a stranger, a dark man, little thin man looks at me. And I said, what do you want? Why did you wake me? What do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Mehr. Nice to meet you. You say Frida or [? Junia ?] is yours. I say she's mine. I said, we will see.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he fell on his knees and started to kiss me. Marry me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I said, I didn't come to marry here. I want to stay father and you mother so [? Junia ?] will have a mother and a father. I said, that's very nice. But I didn't come to marry anyone. We can be friends if you, Mehr, like myself, we'll be friends. And you'll see [? Junia ?] whenever you want to if I take her where I work. You can come to us see her whenever you want. No. If you don't want to marry me, you won't get her. He went back. I haven't seen her. I have been a few days, and I haven't seen her. He hid her somewhere, took her away from there, and hid her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I thought, now I have to do something about it. Where to go? I knew no one there. And I was a stranger there with so many people. And everybody hated me, because I didn't want to marry him. He was serious about.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Get a stranger. And I don't know him at all, who he is and what is in him. Get married. Then I thought, look, to myself, there must be a hospital here and doctors. So many people, there's a hospital. And so I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: I asked someone, could you tell me where the hospital is? I went there. At the first door, I knocked. And there was a doctor. I came in, introduced myself. And I said, I am from [? Filt ?] [? Nuremberg. ?] And through the paper, I found out I had a lost-- I told the story, my husband, the whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: My child was in terrible circumstances. But she's alive. She's here with a man who has the same name as I but no relative at all. And he wants to marry me. Otherwise, he doesn't want to give my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sent the police here. And he hid her. And since I'm here, I haven't seen her even. I can't say anything. Is she mine, or is she not mine? So he says, that is a story. It goes around, we all laugh. And we heard of a man, a Mehr, like you. We heard the story that he once came a woman from somewhere else. And he wants-- we didn't know who it is-- marry her. And she doesn't want to marry. And it goes around. And everybody laughs. I said, now you know the story. That's me. Oh, that's different. Why didn't you come the first thing to us? I said, because I didn't suspect anything. I thought I come for my child and I will get her.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not with those people. He says, you don't know those. We have the battle here with those people. They are killing there. It's a Jewish place after the war, mostly survivors are from Partisans. We had one man killed here-- and drinking. They bring in alcohol. You don't know with whom you are dealing. And I said, no, of course I don't know. All right, don't go back there. We have a room here in the hospital. Stay here. We will do something about it. It came out that 10 men from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] who years ago-- before in Munchen helped me to get back to my name-- remember, I said that when the Hungarian Jews [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That man who knew me from there was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: They set up a tribunal, a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Court.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, court-- to try him. They didn't want to go to the Germans to know about it. They were around Germans. They wouldn't go to a German court, but Jewish. And there were a few lawyers. And there was that. And he laughed at that story when I told him, [? Smolenski ?] from Israel. He was there. And he knew me already from Munchen. And they said, now we'll deal with him. To get a woman comes in-- he wants straight away to marry her because he want her child. And he's so stupid that he believes it's his child. Although she had a different name-- he don't remember the name of his child. Set up a court-- they took him. I haven't seen it. But there, I've seen it. They took in the child, my daughter. It's the first time I have seen her. But of course, so many years, it was a gap in our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, she looked at me. And they ask, it's your mommy? She cried and said, yes, yes. How do you remember your mommy? So she said, when I left, I still had a photo of myself. And I gave her the photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she held-- still remembers now. When I found her, she had the photo carrying always under her dress underneath, she carried. She said, from that photo, I remember my mom. That's my mommy.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say, are you sure? Yes, I am sure. And I remember some marks on her body too. So it's a set up a court and called in the man. They took her first. And they bathed her. And they gave her a dress. There were other children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she slept there, already different, entirely different. And I stayed with them too. And the next day, they called him in. They had to find him. First, they find her. He hid her somewhere. But because he was in police, other policemen knew where he hid her. They found her and then took her over. And they called him in. In the court, I was there. We faced each other. And I never knew him. I looked at him like-- [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: They asked him, what's your wife's name? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He didn't even remember his wife's name. Later, he said something, [? Ruhu ?] or [? Schmuhu. ?] And your name? I said. Your wife had that name? I don't remember. He lied, or he didn't remember. Because he want the child. He lost his child. He wants my child. Many wanted children, you know? So and the child's name? It wasn't another name not like mine, not Frida, not [? Junia. ?] So why do you say it's your daughter? Oh, he acted like this. Or he did it because he wanted the child or he didn't remember. So they said, it's not your child. Then they asked me, what was the name of a woman and her children that your child stayed with during the Germans' occupation? I said, eh, [? Glenbotske. ?] And the children, she played with their children-- [? Christina ?] and [? Vladik ?] or what it was their name.

speaker: interviewee
text: They ask her. And she said, yes, [? Glenbotske. ?] Yes, [? Christina. ?] Because she lived for a few years with them until the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they said, it's not your child. It's not your wife. You can't do anything about a marriage. Because if you say it again, they say that you're stupid. She didn't come to marry here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she doesn't look for a husband. She want only her child. The child is not yours. And you won't get her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, but I know it's my child. They said, you can say as many times as you want. The child recognize the mother. And the mother recognize the child. They said, what we can do, we can give you how much do you want-- money? You want to go to United States, or to Israel? We know you want to. He said, I don't want money. I want my child.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't listen to him, gave him I don't know how much then. But he didn't take the money. Next day, he jumped from second floor-- want to commit suicide. But he was alive. Nothing happened to him. And he went out somewhere to the city, bought a huge doll and a golden watch for my daughter. Because he knew that I leave. Then they said, you talk to her. Maybe you can visit her if you want to travel, visit her before you leave. Go to United States. All right. But I didn't want the watch from him. I said, give it back. It's a golden watch. I don't want anything from you. The doll, she will keep. And come to see us. You can see. If you want to see Frida or [? Junia, ?] you can see her. All right, I went back to [? Filt Nuremberg ?] with her. And she changed overnight, because I looked after her entirely different than there. And I bought her clothing and all kinds of-- and changed like overnight. Two weeks later, he came. He's here. As he came, she already went to-- it was a Hebrew school. They had a school. In Germany, the school-- it's a Yiddish, Jewish school. It wasn't really a school. He came. As he came, she said, mommy I go to school now. And people talk about it that I had a different daddy. That's not my daddy. He's not-- he wants to go out with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was-- you could walk around there, a garden. And he said, come. I'll take you out a little. We go out. She said no, I don't want to go. Why? Because I am ashamed. I had two daddies. So he went back. He was very sad. But he had the girl. She didn't want-- she didn't want to tell anybody that she stayed with him as a father. I explained to her what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he went back. And I think he went to United State. I don't know. I haven't heard of him anymore. But that's was the story how I found her. But before I found her, still in Germany, I used to work night duty, mostly night time. And during the day, the doctor gave me money. And I traveled. Because there were established-- hundreds and hundreds of orphaned children that were found out, you know, like my daughter, survived. Others survived. And I didn't know about it then. Hundreds of people-- of children, older children, younger children survived in Polish homes or in churches-- not churches, but with nuns-- a lot of them. And later, they established orphanages in Germany for Jewish children that were supported by Israel. And its was illegal, because Israel was still not independent. [? Shaleachs ?] took them to Israel, all children. I have seen later a lot of children in Israel. They came as orphanages-- from orphanages. And so [? Shaleachs ?] helped to take them illegal to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were orphanage. Before I found out that she's there-- I didn't tell it before because I wanted to finish that story-- I used to-- I have heard of such orphanages. And I thought, perhaps my child in one of those orphanages before I put into the papers. So I told the doctor that. So he said it's the best thing to go from one orphanage to another one. But they were in different places. I had to travel. One was in Rosenheim, the other one-- not far, but travel by train from place to place. Didn't have a car to travel from place.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were scattered orphanages with hundreds of children that the parents were killed and the children-- I knew quite a few I met in Israel later. And the children were saved by Polands-- by Poles or by churches. A lot were like that-- children survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to such orphanages. I went to one. Night time, I had duty. During the day, I traveled. I came in one. And it was in Rosenheim, not far from where I lived. There was an orphanage. I don't know how many children-- could be 20, 30 children. And they played in a garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- the kindergarten [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] person who worked there, I told her. And she said, look, if you don't the child-- I said, I know the name. So she said, had a look on the list. We haven't got such a name. And some children we haven't got a name at all. Because we found them and the child couldn't tell the name, because they were brought up as little. They didn't know the name. So we have children here, they have no names. Go around and look around if you find one. I went around. And one child-- because I didn't remember her. I remember her how she was at the hospital, thin without hair. And I look at one. And I thought, oh, that must be my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I went up and said, what's your name? Are you [? Vladka? ?] Because [? Vladka ?] was her Christian name. And she didn't answer me. She couldn't speak. Looked at me, and I know. Another child, I said-- and I thought, no. I can't see anyone like my daughter. And I went out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to her, I can't see any child. I went out. And then I said, no. I go back. I can't leave. Maybe my child is here and I leave and never come back. I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And said, why, did you forget something? I said, no. I have to make sure. I have to have a look again at the children. And it was evening. And I had to go back. And I had another look. And then I said, no, no, no. I can see her. I don't-- maybe I don't recognize her. I can't see her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to another orphanage. It's another-- it's the same thing-- until I've seen about three orphanages. And I haven't seen her, because she wasn't there. She was there where I found her later. Later, I gave that advertisement-- and how I found her. And that's how the story how I found her. And then I worked there until all those DP camps people left for other countries. But I had to work from-- because I worked with the UNRA, you know, the international-- I have the paper from there. Because I haven't got a paper from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], because they sent me-- I couldn't leave. They sent me where they wanted, you know, to work. So they sent me to Feldafing. And I have some from Feldafing certificates too. I went to work in Feldafing. And from Feldafing, when the people all left and there was no more people, then they said, now you can go wherever you want. And because I worked in the Red Cross, I could go to settle in England, United States. I never thought of Australia. I thought rather United States or Israel. I wanted to go to Israel. And when Israel was already independent, then I could go legally. Before, it was illegal. But when I told my daughter-- she was already a little girl, not a baby-- she said, no, mommy. No. I won't go there. There are wars. And the Germans didn't kill us. Someone else will kill us. So I didn't go to Israel. And because a few friends went to Australia-- I had no one anywhere, or I had someone, but distant-- I thought, I'll go to-- I didn't know much about Australia. I knew about Australia, you know, and about kangaroos and snakes. But that's all that I knew about Australia. I didn't study Australia or the people in Australia. And that's why we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave Germany to go to Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- you mean, Feldafing was the last. In Feldafing-- I arrived in '51. That was actually begin of '51-- no, New Year of '51, I arrived. In Sydney, I arrived. Yes, I remember exactly. It was Christmas in '50 when I arrived. That means I left end of '50.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you find your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you find your daughter? When was it that you found your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you find your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean, back the story in Germany? I think it was '40-- '47, begin of '47 when Israel got independent.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '47-- yes, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was--

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was already a few years old

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it winter '47?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's for sure. I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beginning of '47 or end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Somewhere begin, yes, in '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first time you saw your daughter for about four years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since she was left in Germany when I got through that, like, [?arbeit?] [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] that camp that they sent us to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did she leave her Polish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's the story I wanted to tell you, yes, how she left. Well, she didn't leave. She would never go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That woman who came and told me I had the news, not the first in Kudowa, but near there-- she left me. She called all people in, come and see God's wonders. Eh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] see the [PAUSES FOR 2 SECONDS] miracles, God's miracles. Because I came and she recognized me straight away as the mother of my daughter-- that I survived and the daughter, and I am looking-- because they never heard of something like this. It was a story written in all papers in Israel, how I found my daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll have to continue on next tape. Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
